turns.content: I need some advice on helping a kid with reading. Bunjil is really struggling with his homework and I am not sure how to help without making it worse.
I'd be happy to help. Can you tell me a bit more — how old is Bunjil, and what kind of reading is he struggling with? Is it decoding words, understanding what he's read, or something else?

He is 10. He can read simple words fine but when the sentences get longer he loses track. Starts guessing at words instead of reading them. And if you ask him what the page was about he cannot tell you. He just gets frustrated and wants to stop.

That's actually a really common pattern at his age. He's moved past the basic decoding stage but hasn't built up fluency yet, so longer sentences use up all his concentration on individual words and there's nothing left for comprehension. The frustration is understandable — he knows he should be able to do it, which makes it worse.

A few things that can help:

- **Read aloud together.** You read a sentence, he reads the next one. This takes the pressure off and lets him hear what fluent reading sounds like.
- **Don't correct every word immediately.** If he guesses wrong but it's close, let him finish the sentence and then go back. Constant correction kills confidence.
- **After each page, ask one simple question** — not a test, just "what happened on that page?" Keep it low-stakes.

What kind of books is he being assigned?
The school sends home chapter books. They are not bad stories but the text is small and the chapters are long. He looks at the page and I can see him shut down before he starts. Too many words.

That visual overwhelm is real for kids who aren't fluent yet. A wall of text is intimidating. Some practical things you can try:

- **Use a piece of card or a ruler** to cover the lines below the one he's reading. It narrows his focus to just one line at a time.
- **Break the chapter into smaller chunks.** Instead of "read chapter 3," try "let's just read to the bottom of this page." Small goals feel achievable.
- **Let him choose some of his own reading too** — even if it's easier than what school assigns. Comics, graphic novels, fact books about animals — anything he actually wants to read builds fluency without feeling like homework.
The card idea is proper good. I will try that tonight. He does like animals. He knows more about the birds and lizards around here than most adults. He just does not connect that with reading.

That's a great opening. If he's knowledgeable about local wildlife, try finding him books or even online articles about the specific animals he's interested in. When a kid already knows the subject matter, reading becomes easier because they can use their background knowledge to predict and make sense of the text. It also shows him that reading is a tool for learning more about things he already cares about, not just a school task.
That makes sense. He can tell you every bird on the billabong but put it in a book and he would struggle to read the names. Maybe I should find some field guides pitched at his level.

A junior field guide would be perfect. Something with pictures, short descriptions, and words he can connect to things he's actually seen. The Australian Museum and some state wildlife agencies publish junior-level guides. There are also apps like the CSIRO bird finder that have text alongside images — if he has access to a tablet, that can work well because he can tap on words he doesn't know.

He does not have a tablet but the school has some. I will ask his teacher about using one for reading. The other thing is I feel bad because I am proper tired by the time homework comes around in the evening. I do not always have the patience to sit with him as long as he needs.

Don't be hard on yourself for that. The fact that you're thinking about how to help him better already puts him in a good position. A few things to consider:

- **Even 10-15 minutes of focused reading together is enough.** Quality matters more than quantity. If you can do that consistently, it makes a real difference over time.
- **Try doing it earlier** if evenings are hard — right after school or after dinner before tiredness really sets in.
- **Audiobooks paired with the physical book** can also help. He follows along in the book while listening. It builds fluency without needing someone to sit with him the whole time.
The audiobook idea could work for the nights when I am spent. Do you know if there are free ones or is it all paid.

There are several free options:

- **Libby/BorrowBox** — most Australian public libraries offer free audiobook borrowing through these apps. You just need a library card, which you can usually sign up for online.
- **Storyline Online** — free website with picture books read aloud by actors. Good for younger readers.
- **Audible** has a free trial, and some children's titles rotate through for free.
- **YouTube** has a surprising number of children's books read aloud, though quality varies.

The library app is your best long-term option. If you can get a library card for the nearest branch, Bunjil would have access to a big catalogue at no cost.

I have a library card for the Katherine library. I do not get in there much but I have the card. I will look into the app. That would be proper good for out here where we cannot get to a library easily.

That's perfect — the Katherine library should give you access to the digital borrowing apps. You can download audiobooks and ebooks over wifi when you have a connection, then they're available offline. So even when you're cut off from town, Bunjil can still access them. Worth setting up on whatever device you have at home.
I will sort that out this week. Thank you. I just want to make sure he does not fall behind and then stop trying altogether. He is a smart kid, he just needs a different way in.

That's exactly right — it's not about ability, it's about finding the approach that clicks for him. Start with the card trick and the animal books, keep sessions short and low-pressure, and get the audiobook app set up as a backup for the tired evenings. He'll build confidence over time. The fact that he's curious and knows so much about the world around him is a real strength to build on.
